## [00:00 - 02:59]

What we're going to talk about is determining OTE and comp structures. So what we're going to cover is the purpose of compensation structures for sales. What is on-track earnings, OTE? Why do we figure out OTE on-track earnings before designing our comp structure? And then how to calculate this for your setters and closers and then reverse engineer your comp structure from there. Now, we're going to talk about all that stuff, which is kind of like the thinking and philosophy behind this. But if you just want to know what we do and what 90% of the industry does, I'll tell you like exact templates of comp structure if you want me to, okay? So that's going to be at the very end. But first, I want to teach you how to think about this. I'm going to teach you the philosophy behind this. So first, purpose of the comp structure. So purpose of compensation structure for sales is to incentivize proper behavior. Okay, I'll repeat that. It's very important. The purpose of sales compensation structure for your setters and closers is to incentivize proper behavior. Incentivize proper behavior. Now, what's proper behavior? Okay, well, proper behavior is one, new business development. Okay, new business development, which means making new front end sales. It's not fulfillment. It's not operations. It's not admin. It's not your CRM. No, the only thing they should be incentivized to do, compensated to do, is to generate new business. That's it. Okay? Now, I do usually recommend splitting your front end sales people from your back end sales people. However, there's times where you're just in a transition period, where you're going to have your front end people do both and don't get down on yourself if you're there. But eventually, at scale, I either like to see a dedicated back end sales person, dedicated inside sales person, or an account management model. We did that for our B2B, where they're a sound account manager and the account manager is incentivized and commissioned on the retention and upsell of the client. Okay? But sometimes you're just in a place where you're just not there yet, and you don't have that inside sales person. And maybe your model doesn't really work with account management. So because of that, it's not the worst thing in the world, just to have your front end sales people handling the back end sales for the client until you can get that in place. That's fine. Okay? Now, another reason or another behavior our comp structure wants to incentivize is enrolling the right clients and do the right products. So let's take an example here. Let's say you have an 8K accelerator and a 30K mastermind. The 8K, they get 800 bucks. The 30K, they get 1200 bucks. Well, what do you think they're going to sell? I had a very similar situation where some guy showed me this and he was like, yeah, we want to sell more of the 30K. And I was like, well, they're basically, they're comped more, essentially, to sell the 8K, especially if you pay upon collected, cash collected. Right? And so it's $22,000. They sell a 30K, but they only get 400 more bucks. Like obviously they're just going to sell the 8K. So make sure the comp structure is set up in a way where you're incentivizing them

## [03:00 - 06:04]

to sell the products that you want to sell. Okay? You also want to set up your comp structure in a way that mitigates your risk. So for every sales rep, we hold a three to five K escrow. The way we do this is we withhold 20% of their commission until escrow is met. So let's say, you know, we pay out commission monthly and let's say for whatever reason, I mean, this would be kind of rare for our company, but let's say for whatever reason, the escrow they need is 5K and they only made 3K the first month. That'd be rare, but let's just say that happened. Well, we wouldn't take all of their 3K. We would just take 20%. Okay? And then we're going to keep taking 20% of their monthly commission stops until we build up that 3K. We have 3K for our B2C side because the price point is lower. And we have 5K for our B2B because the price point is higher. Okay? So what that does is we keep that for 90 days after they leave and then we give it back to them. We're contractually obligated to do that. So make sure you know, you don't just not give it back to them. That's not cool. And then that way we're protected on refunds, charge backs, whatever after they leave. And we can make sure that anything that happens with any other clients enrolled, we don't get left hand in the, you know, holding the bag, right? So we do that and then we only pay out on collected cash collected. That helps us mitigate any commission compensation risks when people default, cancel, refund, whatever. So I would only pay out on cash collected. Some people don't do that. I mean, there might be a reason why not to do it, but I can't think of one. I mean, to me, paying out on cash collected, I mean, that just makes sense to me. Now, what is on-track earnings? So OTE stands for on-track earnings. On-track earnings are the dollar amount the set of our closers going to earn this year if they hit KPIs. Okay? So essentially the OTEs is if they hit all their KPIs, here's what they're going to make this year. Okay? Or this month or this week, whatever. It'll be any time frame. Now, closer OTEs are calculated by using these three figures. One, your validated closing percentage. Two, the amount of lead flow provided daily. And three, your commission per sale. Right? So I'll repeat that. Closer OTEs are calculated by one, validated closing percentage. Two, lead flow. Three, commission per sale. Setter OTEs are calculated based on one, validated closing percentage. Two, medium sets per week averaged between all your setters. And three, commission per close set. Okay? Because we only play on closed set. So let's go through an example here. But this is good to understand because these are your three kind of levers to develop your OTEs. So closer example, let's say you consider the following figures. So 25% closing ratio. Let's say that 25% is calculated based on scheduled to close. So we're not even counting. We are counting no shows here. Or sorry, we're not counting no shows here. So this would just be like people who booked versus who closed. Four appointments that we are generating and providing to the closer daily.

And then $750 per unit sold. That's 10% commission at a $7,500 price point. So here's how we would calculate that out based on these metrics. ## [09:11 - 12:11]

So that would be your OTE. So then you got to ask yourself, do you want to bump that up, get a little bit more of a competitive pricing, or a competitive comp structure, and maybe add in a base by doing that? Or maybe do 3.3 or 3.5. I recommend for setters, if this wasn't enough, based on what you wanted to comp the setters, to add in a small 1K a month, 1500 a lot a month base. Or you can pull the other levers. Maybe 25% closing ratio is like, your guys are doing way more than that. We'll calculate it based on 35. Also, maybe they can have more appointments a month. So do this on 20. Then they'll make a lot more just based on that. I do like commission only for setters, but some people do do a base. We'll talk more about that later. Now, let's talk about aligning your OTEs with the talent you want to attract in the marketplace. Sales people have more reasons to work for you than just money. You might go through this section, and if I don't preface it by saying this, you might be like, well, damn, these people, they just want to work just for the money. That's not true. Definitely not true. But money is important. It has to align with what they want, and there's other things that are important as well. We do want to make sure that our OTEs are competitive with what they're also looking at in the marketplace. We're going to want to toy with these levers, our commission per sale, our lead volume, our closing ratio to get our on-track earnings where we want. We also can tier our on-track earnings, and that's what I do. For example, I create three different tiers on the scorecard, which will cover scorecards later. That's the template I use not only to set agreements and expectations of what the position actually is, but also sell the vision and really sell the benefits of the position. Number one, I base the OTEs on the minimum KPI. Number two, I base it on what's reasonably good and what they can achieve with above average performance. And number three, I base the OTEs on what's actually possible. What they can achieve with true world-class A-player performance. That way, let's say we go back to this example, and you're like, damn, 66K a year, that's good for a setter, but I kind of want a setter who's top tier. I want my setters to be able to make 80, 100 grand a year and more. This could be your number one, and then your number two could be based on a few higher metrics in terms of appointment set and also closing ratio. That maybe gets them to 80, your tier three maybe gets them to 110. Right now, they can kind of see the vision of like, oh, it's totally possible for me to hit 110, but they know based on minimum KPI, they're going to have to make 66K a year just to stay on the team because that's the KPI's. So as you'll see, that allows me to keep my comp structure in check while recruiting the highest tier closers and setters, respectively. Now, let's talk about the four tiers of high ticket salespeople. I think this is just an interesting framework that I created. And so as you're developing your comp structure and your OTEs,

## [12:11 - 15:14]

and you're trying to figure out, okay, well, what type of talent do I want to attract? I kind of broke it out into four different buckets of salespeople that are in the recruiting marketplace right now. Tier one is a setter role, okay? In a potential 40 to 60K a year, this is going to be pretty entry level, like, experience-wise, they're probably not going to have any, but what they are going to have is hunger and drive, okay? Best for like B2C, health-dating, personal development type offers. You can certainly do it for B2B as well. Tier two is a setter role, and the income potential is going to be 60 to 120K a year. So all my setters I would classify as tier two, okay? Primarily going to be B2B offers, but certainly doesn't have to be experience. They should have some experience. It could be setting, or most likely it's just going to be in the online space, where they used to have a business, or they, you know, maybe did some work for another company in a different capacity, whatever, right? You could also have somebody with no experience, but just great attitude, great hunger, especially for setting, drive and hunger and passion is 85%, okay? Given you lead, you train well, you give them all the stuff, right? So a lot of my guys, I try to get a tier two. Now there's also tier three closers. This is 70K to 150K a year. So this could be really any industry, B2C, B2B, done for you services. Experience-wise, you do want to have some previous experience here, a high ticket or not a non-high ticket. Previous experience in business is good as well. Like when I got started in the closing, for instance, I never had closing experience, but I had closing experience for my agency. So I was familiar with the online space. I had done about 300 sales calls, which is decent. I guess you do call that high ticket sales experience. And I'd run my own business, so I was experienced, but I'd never been a full-time high ticket sales person, right? Previous setting experience. So a lot of all-star setters will want to move into kind of tier three here. So this is good for B2C, B2B, done for you. Here's kind of the experience levels that you're going to be looking at. Now tier four is anywhere from 200 to 500K a year plus, okay? Most of the times you're going to be looking in like the 15 to 20 grand a month range or more. But if you're in 15 to 20, you can get tier four, okay? 15 to 20s where a lot of these closers want to be. So this is going to be a lot of B2B, a lot of professional services. I'm not saying if you have a personal development or a B2C offer, you can't get in this range. You could, but generally B2B and done for you services a lot of times. Experience-wise, they're going to have solid experience in directly high ticket or a different, very high-performance sales industry. Like sometimes door-to-door guys can come in and just crush high ticket. Time-sharer guys come in, they crush high ticket. So that's definitely possible. Solid sales experience from running their own business. I guess you could have maybe qualified me in tier four potentially. And also like all-star, just amazing setters could kind of jump tier three into tier four. So this is kind of how I categorize the different salespeople out there. And just know you want to develop your on-track earnings to where you can try to get into these ranges as much as you can.

## [15:14 - 18:03]

I don't think necessarily paying four or 500 grand a month for a closer in most situations is even necessary. Even above 300 on a lot of situations, not always necessary. I think in the 30s is like the top tier of where I would usually pay. But you will, especially if you're looking for closers, 15 to 20-ish plus is going to get you some good people. For setters, 80, 80-ish, and obviously you can go up to 100, 120. That potential can be really, really good. And it can be all commission-based. So they're only going to eat what they kill. Now, let's talk about adjusting your compensation structure slash OTEs to fit your targeting tier here. So again, don't feel like you have to recruit tier two and tier four. There's a lot of times where it's actually best to go tier one or tier three. So many offers are fine in other tiers and it's actually preferred because they get the job done, they're cost-effective for your business, they work within your margins, etc. There's a lot of B2C businesses where tier one setters are like, that's all you need and they're going to crush. For setters, if you need to adjust the structure, you can pay more per sale, you can add a small base or a drop, or you could validate a higher closing ratio. For closers, you could pay a higher percentage per sale, fix your leaf flow, validate a higher closing ratio. So now, I'm going to show you my comp structure, which probably a lot of you guys are like, okay, this is actually what I wanted to get into at the very end here. You can model mine, just make sure you do think about all this philosophy before you get into that, just to make sure this is actually what you want to do. So basically for closers, we do 10% commissions paid out on collected, 500 bonus per 50k generated, 200 per pan full. Setters, we do 3% commission, no base. However, many times I see a $1,500 a month base. I think that's fine. My setters just have such high earning potential that to me, I'm just, I just rather than be total commissioned and have a base for what reason? Like the base, I want to again, have their comp structure incentivize them on the proper behavior. Like the base doesn't do shit. So I just have 3% and 10%, but if you do have like a new setting position, or your OTs aren't that good, don't be scared of the base. I just personally don't do it. I do 3%, I do 10%.
